content:
Klint reports this warning when I'm trying to integrate it into upstream:

warning: stack size of `gsp::cmdq::Cmdq::new` is 8216 bytes, exceeds the 2048-byte limit
   --> drivers/gpu/nova-core/gsp/cmdq.rs:453:5
    |
453 |     pub(crate) fn new(dev: &device::Device<device::Bound>) -> Result<Cmdq> {
    |     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
    |
    = note: the stack size is inferred from instruction `sub $0x2018,%rsp` at .text+32778
It looks to me that using dma_write! on the PTE array is causing the issue.

Yes, the PteArray is temporarily created on the stack to be written. I guess we can fix this by converting its constructor to return an in-place initializer, but that might require unsafe code to access the coherent memory.
Can the PTE be defined as an array of atomic integers?
Yes, that's what it is actually, but having the structure helps contain the code
Mmm maybe we could change dma_write! to accept an Init instead of a value? Values implement Init so the existing code should not break IIUC
Init does not perform write using volatile.
The current code defines an array of integer and then use dma_write macro. I am asking if this can be array of atomic integers so you can perform relaxed store instead for initialization.
But that alone would not remove the stack object, would it?
We can provide a special zeroing initializer
Another idea: perhaps we can add an dma allocation API that takes init. As the init is executed before a dma object is returned, there's no issue of race at all and can use normal init.
That would probably be best - the question then becomes how to deal with the fact that CoherentAllocations are arrays of objects
An Fn that returns an Init?

I'd be happy to send a patch for this!
I already have a draft here if you're interested: https://github.com/a1phyr/linux/tree/cmdq_init
I prototyped something similar, however I am worried that once DmaAddress is given to the user, they might send this to device, even if CoherentAllocation is yet private.
(I would call the fn new_coherent by the way)
Mmm yeah I wouldn't pass the DmaAddress to the initializer. I guess in the case of Nova we should just initialize the pages one by one using dma_write! since we need that information.
This PteArray thing was probably a bit of overengineering on my end

Gary Guo said:

I prototyped something similar, however I am worried that once DmaAddress is given to the user, they might send this to device, even if CoherentAllocation is yet private.

Is it actually possible with safe code? DmaAdress is a type alias to dma_addr_t (which is basically an integer), so that would mean that safe code would be able to send any address (valid or not) to the device. This seems weird to me.
There is currently nothing preventing you from giving any address to the device to do DMA
(Perhaps this would mean that the register! macro needs to be unsafe?)

@Alexandre Courbot I think I'm done with the implementing part, just left to run the tests. Once that is done, I'll have to figure out the whole git send-email stuff (it's my first time :) )

As for the impelementation, does this look right? 

    fn new(dev: &device::Device<device::Bound>) -> Result<Self> {
        const MSGQ_SIZE: u32 = num::usize_into_u32::<{ size_of::<Msgq>() }>();
        const RX_HDR_OFF: u32 = num::usize_into_u32::<{ mem::offset_of!(Msgq, rx) }>();

        let gsp_mem =
            CoherentAllocation::<GspMem>::alloc_coherent(dev, 1, GFP_KERNEL | __GFP_ZERO)?;
        const NUM_PAGES: usize = GSP_PAGE_SIZE / size_of::<u64>();

        // One by one GSP Page write to the memory to avoid stack overflow when allocating
        // the whole array at once.
        let item = gsp_mem.item_from_index(0)?;
        for i in 0..NUM_PAGES {
            let pte_value = gsp_mem
                    .dma_handle()
                    .checked_add(num::usize_as_u64(i) << GSP_PAGE_SHIFT)
                    .ok_or(EOVERFLOW)?;

            // SAFETY: `item_from_index` ensures that `item` is always a valid pointer and can be
            // dereferenced. The compiler also further validates the expression on whether `field`
            // is a member of `item` when expanded by the macro.
            //
            // Further, this is dma_write! macro expanded and modified to allow for individual
            // page write.
            unsafe {
                let ptr_field = core::ptr::addr_of_mut!((*item).ptes[i]);
                gsp_mem.field_write(ptr_field, pte_value);
            }
        }

        dma_write!(gsp_mem[0].cpuq.tx = MsgqTxHeader::new(MSGQ_SIZE, RX_HDR_OFF, MSGQ_NUM_PAGES))?;
        dma_write!(gsp_mem[0].cpuq.rx = MsgqRxHeader::new())?;

        Ok(Self(gsp_mem))
    }

I also changed the LogBuffer::new() in a similar manner as it was also using the PteArray struct.

Also, for the testing part, I just need to run the unit tests or something else?
something like that, but you want to use dma_write instead of field_write.

for testing, if you have a working GPU you can check that the driver still loads, but that's not mandatory - I will test it anyway. More importantly, make sure to follow https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submit-checklist-addendum

also I suggest you use b4 for sending your series. It makes it very difficult to NOT do things correctly. :) If it makes you less nervous, feel free to do a test-drive with my email address only, and I will let you know if it looks good!

For the dma_write, I wanted to write it like that but the macro does not support more than one set of brackets. This is ok gsp_mem[0].ptes = but not this gsp_mem[0].ptes[0] =. I could also be wrong but then I'm not sure. 

error: no rules expected `[`
   --> drivers/gpu/nova-core/gsp/cmdq.rs:224:39
    |
224 |             dma_write!(gsp_mem[0].ptes[i] = pte_value)?;
    |                                       ^ no rules expected this token in macro call
    |
note: while trying to match `=`

That's why I basically unwrapped the macro. Should I modify the dma_write macro? Or that's a too important/central of a change?

And I'll resume on this in a few days, as I have a busy start of the week. Also thank you for being so welcome and making it easy for someone new :)

Alexandre Courbot said:

also I suggest you use b4 for sending your series. It makes it very difficult to NOT do things correctly. :) If it makes you less nervous, feel free to do a test-drive with my email address only, and I will let you know if it looks good!

Ah, I see. There is some rework going on with dma_write anyway, so maybe we can improve things afterwards. @Gary Guo for visibility.

Don't hesitate to send your first patch without overthinking it too much, getting feedback is part of the process. And of course take your time - appreciate your willingness to spend the time to look into this!
Just to confirm, should I leave it as is then or add another case to dma_write. I can at least add a todo and reference the rework. And I'll be sending it soon then!
Leave it as-is for now, dma_write is a moving target so we will see whether the pending changes cover this use-case as well.

Index projection on DMA write will be supported in the upcoming IO projection rework (in fact it is already in my POC tree). Please leave it as it like Alex suggested, if your patch land first I'll convert it
Related: https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/CANiq72mAQxbRJZDnik3Qmd4phvFwPA01O2jwaaXRh_T+2=L-qA@mail.gmail.com/
